0.name: Consolidation & brain stimulation

top_papers.title: Functional MRI evidence for adult motor cortex plasticity during motor skill learning
Neuroplasticity subserving motor skill learning
Noninvasive cortical stimulation enhances motor skill acquisition over multiple days through an effect on consolidation
Reorganization and plasticity in the adult brain during learning of motor skills
Early consolidation in human primary motor cortex

0.summary: This community investigates the neural mechanisms underlying the acquisition, offline consolidation, and transfer of motor skills. Researchers frequently employ non-invasive brain stimulation paradigms—such as tDCS, TMS, and tACS—alongside neuroimaging (fMRI, EEG) to modulate and measure cortical excitability in the primary motor cortex, cerebellum, and posterior parietal cortex. Recurring findings demonstrate that motor learning involves distinct early and late phases, with skill enhancement continuing offline during wakefulness and sleep, and that targeted neuromodulation or moderate aerobic exercise can significantly facilitate neuroplasticity and learning outcomes.

1.name: Motor Adaptation

top_papers.title: Adaptive representation of dynamics during learning of a motor task
Interacting adaptive processes with different timescales underlie short-term motor learning
Error correction, sensory prediction, and adaptation in motor control
Consolidation in human motor memory
An implicit plan overrides an explicit strategy during visuomotor adaptation

1.summary: This community investigates how the human nervous system adapts motor commands in response to environmental perturbations, such as mechanical force fields, visuomotor rotations, and asymmetric treadmills. Researchers frequently employ reaching and walking paradigms to study the mechanisms of sensorimotor adaptation, focusing on sensory prediction errors, internal model formation, and the interplay between implicit and explicit learning. Recurring findings demonstrate that the central nervous system rapidly updates internal models to compensate for errors, and that the retention and generalization of these adaptations across limbs or tasks can be significantly modulated by factors like working memory load, balance challenges, and the specific tuning of muscle synergies.

2.name: Cerebellum: synaptic plasticity

top_papers.title: Cerebellar long-term depression: Characterization, signal transduction, and functional roles
The cerebellum and the adaptive coordination of movement
Distributed synergistic plasticity and cerebellar learning
The cerebellum: A neuronal learning machine?
Beyond parallel fiber LTD: The diversity of synaptic and non-synaptic plasticity in the cerebellum

2.summary: This community investigates the neural mechanisms of motor learning and cognitive function within the cerebellum, focusing heavily on Purkinje cells and their synaptic plasticity. Researchers employ diverse paradigms—including vestibulo-ocular reflexes, eyeblink conditioning, and novel load adaptation in animal models—alongside in vivo electrophysiology, genetic manipulations, and human lesion studies. A recurring finding is that cerebellar learning relies on multiple plasticity mechanisms, such as long-term depression (LTD) mediated by climbing fiber inputs and structural axonal sprouting, which collectively enable predictive motor control, error correction, and non-motor memory formation.

3.name: Contextual Interference

top_papers.title: A schema theory of discrete motor skill learning
Challenge Point: A Framework for Conceptualizing the Effects of Various Practice Conditions in Motor Learning
Knowledge of results and motor learning: A review and critical reappraisal
A closed-loop theory of motor learning
Principles derived from the study of simple skills do not generalize to complex skill learning

3.summary: This community investigates how practice organization and feedback variables influence the acquisition, retention, and transfer of motor skills. Researchers utilize pre-post retention and transfer paradigms to test the effects of contextual interference (random versus blocked practice schedules), differential training, and varying frequencies or timings of augmented feedback. Recurring findings demonstrate that while structured practice improves baseline performance, introducing variability—such as random practice schedules or reduced/fading feedback frequencies—often enhances intertask transfer, motor automaticity, and long-term skill retention across tasks ranging from fine motor coordination to prosthetic use.

4.name: Focus Of Attention

top_papers.title: Optimizing performance through intrinsic motivation and attention for learning: The OPTIMAL theory of motor learning
Attentional focus and motor learning: A review of 15 years
Motor skill learning and performance: A review of influential factors
The automaticity of complex motor skill learning as a function of attentional focus
Instructions for motor learning: Differential effects of internal versus external focus of attention

4.summary: This community investigates how instructional strategies, feedback mechanisms, and attentional focus influence motor skill acquisition and performance across diverse populations. Researchers frequently employ experimental paradigms comparing external versus internal focus instructions, implicit versus explicit learning, and self-controlled versus yoked feedback using retention tests and kinematic analyses. Recurring findings indicate that promoting an external focus of attention, utilizing implicit or analogy learning to reduce working memory load, and providing learner autonomy consistently enhance motor learning, movement efficiency, and coordination variability.
themes: External vs. internal focus of attention

5.name: Action Observation

top_papers.title: THE REPRESENTING BRAIN - NEURAL CORRELATES OF MOTOR INTENTION AND IMAGERY
Motor learning by observing
EFFECTS OF MENTAL PRACTICE ON MOTOR SKILL LEARNING AND PERFORMANCE A META ANALYSIS
Formation of a motor memory by action observation
Action observation has a positive impact on rehabilitation of motor deficits after stroke

5.summary: This community investigates how motor skills are acquired and consolidated through non-physical practice, specifically focusing on action observation (AO) and motor imagery (MI). Researchers utilize paradigms like visuomotor adaptation, sequential timing tasks, and transcranial magnetic stimulation to evaluate neural mechanisms such as mirror neuron system activation and motor resonance. Recurring findings demonstrate that while AO and MI share neural substrates with physical execution and effectively facilitate motor learning, combining them with each other, neurofeedback, or mixed expert-novice models significantly enhances skill acquisition and kinematics in both healthy individuals and clinical populations.
themes: Mirror Neuron System and Motor Resonance

6.name: Motor Cortex

top_papers.title: Rapid formation and selective stabilization of synapses for enduring motor memories
Functional reorganization of the rat motor cortex following motor skill learning
Cortical Synaptogenesis and Motor Map Reorganization Occur during Late, but Not Early, Phase of Motor Skill Learning
LEARNING CAUSES SYNAPTOGENESIS WHEREAS MOTOR ACTIVITY CAUSES ANGIOGENESIS IN CEREBELLAR CORTEX OF ADULT RATS
Differential corticostriatal plasticity during fast and slow motor skill learning in mice

6.summary: This community investigates the cellular and circuit-level mechanisms of neuroplasticity underlying motor skill learning, primarily focusing on the primary motor cortex and its interactions with neuromodulatory systems. Using rodent models engaged in skilled behaviors like forelimb reaching and running wheel tasks, researchers employ advanced techniques such as optogenetics, in vivo calcium imaging, epidural stimulation, and dendritic spine tracking. Recurring findings demonstrate that motor learning induces rapid, task-specific synaptic reorganization and motor map expansion, processes that are tightly regulated by dopamine and noradrenaline and can be harnessed via stimulation or rehabilitation to rescue motor deficits in conditions like stroke, dystonia, and fetal alcohol spectrum disorders.

7.name: Stroke: robotic & VR rehab

top_papers.title: Motor learning: Its relevance to stroke recovery and neurorehabilitation
Augmented visual, auditory, haptic, and multimodal feedback in motor learning: A review
Evaluation of robotic training forces that either enhance or reduce error in chronic hemiparetic stroke survivors
Review of control strategies for robotic movement training after neurologic injury.
Motor learning elicited by voluntary drive

7.summary: This community investigates motor learning and neurorehabilitation strategies for individuals recovering from central nervous system injuries, particularly stroke and hemiparesis. Researchers frequently employ robotic-mediated training, virtual reality, and varied feedback modalities—such as haptic guidance, error amplification, and visual cues—to facilitate repetitive and rewarding motor practice. Recurring findings demonstrate that mixed practice schedules (combining unassisted and robot-guided trials) and augmented haptic feedback significantly improve movement kinematics, sensory recovery, and neuroplasticity, suggesting that integrating these technologies with conventional therapy optimizes rehabilitation outcomes.

8.name: Ecological Dynamics

top_papers.title: Representative learning design and functionality of research and practice in sport
Perceptual-cognitive expertise in sport: A meta-analysis
Practice, instruction and skill acquisition in soccer: Challenging tradition
Skill acquisition in sport: Some applications of an evolving practice ecology
A constraints-led perspective to understanding skill acquisition and game play: A basis for integration of motor learning theory and physical education praxis?

8.summary: This community investigates motor skill acquisition and athletic development through the lens of ecological dynamics and the constraints-led approach, focusing on how practice environments can be designed to optimize learning. Researchers employ methods such as equipment modification, video-based temporal occlusion tests, and qualitative interviews to evaluate paradigms like representative learning design, opposed practice, and deliberate practice. Recurring findings demonstrate that skill development, decision-making, and anticipation are best enhanced in context-specific, "alive" environments that promote movement variability and problem-solving, rather than through the isolated repetition of ideal movement patterns.

9.name: Skill Acquisition

top_papers.title: Acquisition of cognitive skill
Determinants of Individual Differences During Skill Acquisition: Cognitive Abilities and Information Processing
The power law repealed: The case for an exponential law of practice
Expert and Exceptional Performance: Evidence of Maximal Adaptation to Task Constraints
MOTIVATION AND COGNITIVE-ABILITIES - AN INTEGRATIVE APTITUDE TREATMENT INTERACTION APPROACH TO SKILL ACQUISITION

9.summary: This community investigates the cognitive and perceptual-motor mechanisms underlying skill acquisition, practice, and transfer across the lifespan. Researchers utilize complex paradigms like high-fidelity simulators, computer games, and logic tasks, analyzing performance trajectories through power functions, negative exponential models, and latent growth modeling. A recurring finding is that the transition from effortful, declarative processing to proceduralized, memory-based performance is heavily moderated by individual differences in working memory, spatial ability, deliberate practice goals, and age-related strategy shifts.

10.name: Coordination

top_papers.title: Motor skill acquisition
Free(z)ing degrees of freedom in skill acquisition
Time scales in motor learning and development
Learning and stability: a psychophysiological analysis of a case of motor learning with clinical applications
Learning as change of coordination dynamics: Theory and experiment

10.summary: This community investigates how the human motor system manages abundant degrees of freedom and movement variability during skill acquisition, frequently framing this challenge through Bernstein's problem and ecological-dynamical perspectives. Researchers employ methods like uncontrolled manifold (UCM) analysis, dynamical systems modeling, and kinematic tracking across complex tasks such as bimanual pointing, postural control, and wheelchair propulsion. A recurring finding is that motor learning involves transitioning from rigid control to adaptive variability, where the central nervous system forms coordinative structures that selectively stabilize critical task variables and unfreeze degrees of freedom.

11.name: Parkinson's Disease

top_papers.title: A neostriatal habit learning system in humans
Motor learning in Parkinson's disease: limitations and potential for rehabilitation
Neuropsychological evidence for multiple implicit memory systems: a comparison of Alzheimer's, Huntington's, and Parkinson's disease patients.
Motor procedural learning in Parkinson's disease
Exercise-enhanced neuroplasticity targeting motor and cognitive circuitry in Parkinson's disease

11.summary: This community investigates motor and implicit skill learning in aging populations and neurodegenerative disorders, with a primary focus on Parkinson's disease (PD). Researchers utilize paradigms like the Serial Reaction Time Task, probabilistic classification, and visuo-motor adaptation to demonstrate how neostriatal damage impairs habit formation, sequence learning, and skill retention compared to healthy aging or other conditions like Alzheimer's disease. Recurring findings highlight that while PD patients exhibit slower learning rates and reduced neural efficiency, their motor learning and retention can be significantly enhanced through targeted interventions such as aerobic exercise, repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS), virtual reality, and external sensory cueing.

12.name: Microsurgery

top_papers.title: Teaching Surgical Skills â€” Changes in the Wind.
Teaching surgical skills: What kind of practice makes perfect? A randomized, controlled trial
Proving the value of simulation in laparoscopic surgery
Laparoscopic training on bench models: Better and more cost effective than operating room experience?
Psychomotor testing predicts rate of skill acquisition for proficiency-based laparoscopic skills training

12.summary: This community investigates the acquisition and assessment of technical motor skills in surgical and microsurgical education outside the operating room. Researchers employ randomized controlled trials and performance tracking to evaluate various simulation modalities, including immersive virtual reality, home-based tablet trainers, cadaveric models, and computer-based video training, using standardized metrics like OSATS. Recurring findings demonstrate that structured simulation curricula significantly accelerate early skill acquisition and improve initial clinical performance compared to conventional training, though evidence for long-term transfer to real-life patient outcomes remains limited.

13.name: Perceptual Motor Learning

top_papers.title: Benefits of multisensory learning
The multifaceted interplay between attention and multisensory integration
Pip and Pop: Nonspatial Auditory Signals Improve Spatial Visual Search.
Perceptual Training Narrows the Temporal Window of Multisensory Binding.
Neural Correlates of Multisensory Integration of Ecologically Valid Audiovisual Events.

13.summary: This community investigates the mechanisms of multisensory integration, focusing on how the brain combines cross-modal inputs (such as audiovisual and visuotactile stimuli) to shape perception, learning, and memory. Researchers employ behavioral psychophysics, EEG/ERPs, and fMRI to test spatial and temporal rules, utilizing paradigms like simultaneity judgments, cross-modal illusions, and expert-novice comparisons. Recurring findings indicate that multisensory integration operates across multiple neural stages, relies on temporal binding windows that mature into adolescence, and can significantly enhance or impair subsequent unimodal processing depending on prior expertise and stimulus congruency.

14.name: Vocal Learning

top_papers.title: Contribution of an avian basal ganglia-forebrain circuit to real-time modulation of song
A basal ganglia-forebrain circuit in the songbird biases motor output to avoid vocal errors
What songbirds teach us about learning
How sleep affects the developmental learning of bird song.
A hypothesis for basal ganglia-dependent reinforcement learning in the songbird

14.summary: This community investigates the neural, behavioral, and molecular mechanisms of sensorimotor vocal learning, primarily using songbirds like zebra finches as a model for human speech acquisition. Researchers employ behavioral tracking, electrophysiology, molecular profiling, and computational modeling to understand how auditory feedback guides motor output and error correction. Recurring findings highlight the critical role of cortical-basal ganglia circuits in driving real-time vocal modulation, experience-dependent intrinsic plasticity, and developmentally regulated long-term potentiation. Additionally, studies demonstrate that vocal memory consolidation is performance-dependent and occurs during daytime practice, while factors like FoxP2 expression and lateralized brain activation during sleep further regulate learning proficiency.

15.name: Cerebral Palsy

top_papers.title: Efficacy of a hand-arm bimanual intensive therapy (HABIT) in children with hemiplegic cerebral palsy: A randomized control trial
A reflection on motor learning theory in pediatric occupational therapy practice
Effects of constraint-induced movement therapy in young children with hemiplegic cerebral palsy: an adapted model.
Hand and Arm Bimanual Intensive Therapy Including Lower Extremity (HABIT-ILE) in Children With Unilateral Spastic Cerebral Palsy: A Randomized Trial
Effectiveness of motor interventions in infants with cerebral palsy: a systematic review

15.summary: This community investigates motor learning interventions to improve bimanual coordination, gross motor function, and mobility in children and infants with cerebral palsy. Researchers evaluate task-focused therapies—such as Hand-Arm Bimanual Intensive Therapy (HABIT), robotic-assisted gait training, and virtual environments—using randomized controlled trials, longitudinal cohorts, and scoping reviews. Recurring findings demonstrate that intensive, activity-focused motor learning coaching significantly enhances the retention and transfer of motor skills, though qualitative studies reveal that clinical implementation is often hindered by physical therapists' limited theoretical understanding and inconsistent reporting of motor learning strategies in existing protocols.

16.name: Developmental Coordination Disorder

top_papers.title: Brain activation associated with motor skill practice in children with developmental coordination disorder: An fMRI study
Computational approaches to motor control
Compromised motor control in children with DCD: A deficit in the internal model?-A systematic review
Impaired visuo-motor sequence learning in Developmental Coordination Disorder
Developmental coordination disorder: A review and update

16.summary: This community investigates the neural, cognitive, and biomechanical mechanisms underlying Developmental Coordination Disorder (DCD) in children, focusing on deficits in motor learning, postural control, and visuomotor adaptation. Researchers employ paradigms such as fMRI, force plate balance assessments, graphic tablet handwriting tasks, and visual feedback rotation to compare children with DCD to typically developing peers. Recurring findings indicate that children with DCD exhibit atypical activation in cerebellar-parietal networks, poorly defined internal models, and impaired cognitive-motor integration, which manifest as greater movement variability and slower sensory processing. Consequently, evidence-based interventions emphasize explicit cognitive problem-solving, such as the CO-OP approach, rather than mere repetitive practice to improve daily motor performance.

17.name: Pain

top_papers.title: The effects of intra-oral pain on motor cortex neuroplasticity associated with short-term novel tongue-protrusion training in humans
One hour of tongue-task training is associated with plasticity in corticomotor control of the human tongue musculature
Plasticity in corticomotor control of the human tongue musculature induced by tongue-task training
The role of motor learning and neuroplasticity in designing rehabilitation approaches for musculoskeletal pain disorders
Tonic pain experienced during locomotor training impairs retention despite normal performance during acquisition

17.summary: This community investigates how acute pain, chronic musculoskeletal pain, muscle fatigue, and aging impact motor learning, sensorimotor integration, and postural control. Researchers frequently employ transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS), electromyography (EMG), and kinematic tracking to assess corticomotor excitability and motor unit recruitment during novel tasks like gait adaptation, lumbopelvic tilting, and visuomotor tracking. A recurring finding is that nociceptive input and fatigue impair motor skill acquisition and disrupt cortical neuroplasticity, prompting compensatory changes in motor unit recruitment strategies to maintain force. However, targeted interventions, such as sensor-based feedback and short-term motor training, can successfully improve movement control and anticipatory postural adjustments in both clinical and aging populations.

18.name: Sequence Learning

top_papers.title: Implicit motor sequence learning is not purely perceptual
Implicit motor sequence learning is represented in response locations
Levels of analysis in motor control
Explicit knowledge enhances motor vigor and performance: Motivation versus practice in sequence tasks
Implicit learning and statistical learning: one phenomenon, two approaches

18.summary: This community investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying procedural and motor sequence learning, primarily utilizing Serial Reaction Time Tasks (SRTT) alongside EEG, TMS, and complex virtual reality paradigms. A recurring finding is the competitive relationship between executive functions and implicit learning; reducing top-down prefrontal cognitive control—whether through targeted TMS, meditation delays, or long-term alcohol use—often preserves or even enhances the acquisition and retrieval of statistical regularities. Furthermore, research demonstrates that sequence learning is fundamentally motor rather than purely perceptual, and that skill acquisition can be further modulated by explicit knowledge, rhythmic multisensory stimulation, and task integration.

19.name: Striatum

top_papers.title: The basal ganglia and adaptive motor control
Modulation of striatal projection systems by dopamine
Neural mechanisms of reward-related motor learning
Effect of the nigrostriatal dopamine system on acquired neural responses in the striatum of behaving monkeys
Autism-associated neuroligin-3 mutations commonly impair striatal circuits to boost repetitive behaviors

19.summary: This community investigates the molecular and cellular mechanisms of the striatum and corticostriatal circuits in motor learning, reward processing, and behavioral plasticity. Researchers frequently employ transgenic mouse models, electrophysiology, and behavioral assays like the rotarod to study synaptic plasticity (LTP/LTD) and cell-type-specific signaling pathways involving dopamine, acetylcholine, and NMDA receptors. A recurring finding is that disruptions to striatal synaptic transmission and plasticity—whether through genetic mutations like DYT1 and Huntingtin, circadian regulators like Bmal1, or altered neurotransmitter dynamics—underlie motor coordination deficits in neurological conditions such as dystonia, autism, and Parkinson's disease.

20.name: Saccade Adaptation

top_papers.title: The characteristics and neuronal substrate of saccadic eye movement plasticity
Seccadic dysmetria and adaptation after lesions of the cerebellar cortex
Saccadic gain modification: Visual error drives motor adaptation
Reduced saccadic resilience and impaired saccadic adaptation due to cerebellar disease
Cerebellar-dependent motor learning is based on pruning a Purkinje cell population response

20.summary: This community investigates the neural mechanisms of motor learning and error correction in the oculomotor system, focusing primarily on saccadic and convergence eye movement adaptation. Researchers frequently employ intrasaccadic target-step paradigms combined with fMRI, cerebellar lesions, and neuromodulation techniques like tDCS and cTBS to study how the brain compensates for movement dysmetria. Recurring findings highlight the critical role of the posterior cerebellum and midbrain tegmentum in processing predictive error signals to adjust saccade gain, revealing distinct plasticity mechanisms for different types of adaptation, such as inward versus outward target shifts.

21.name: Brain-Computer Interface

top_papers.title: Brain-computer interfaces in neurological rehabilitation
Task-related training improves performance of seated reaching tasks after stroke: A randomized controlled trial
Construction of efficacious gait and upper limb functional interventions based on brain plasticity evidence and model-based measures for stroke patients
Feasibility of a new application of noninvasive brain computer interface (BCI): A case study of training for recovery of volitional motor control after stroke
Comparison of robotics, functional electrical stimulation, and motor learning methods for treatment of persistent upper extremity dysfunction after stroke: A randomized controlled trial

21.summary: This community investigates the use of non-invasive brain-computer interfaces (BCIs) and neurofeedback to drive motor rehabilitation, primarily targeting persistent upper and lower limb deficits in stroke survivors. Researchers employ EEG and fMRI to decode motor imagery and movement intentions, coupling these signals with robotic orthoses, functional electrical stimulation (FES), or non-invasive brain stimulation (tDCS, TMS) to provide real-time sensory and proprioceptive feedback. Recurring findings demonstrate that these closed-loop BCI paradigms successfully induce targeted neuroplasticity, modulate corticospinal excitability, and alter functional brain connectivity, leading to clinically significant improvements in volitional motor control and functional recovery.

22.name: Speech Production

top_papers.title: Neural modeling and imaging of the cortical interactions underlying syllable production
The DIVA model: A neural theory of speech acquisition and production
Sensory preference in speech production revealed by simultaneous alteration of auditory and somatosensory feedback
Simultaneous acquisition of multiple auditory-motor transformations in speech
Perceptual recalibration of speech sounds following speech motor learning

22.summary: This community investigates the neural and behavioral mechanisms of speech motor control, sensorimotor adaptation, and speech sequence learning in both typical speakers and individuals with speech disorders such as stuttering and apraxia. Researchers frequently employ altered auditory feedback paradigms, motor-sequence learning tasks, and neuromodulation (rTMS, tDCS) to understand how sensory feedback integrates with motor planning. Recurring findings indicate that precise auditory feedback is crucial for speech motor learning, and that targeted sensorimotor adaptation or neuromodulation can improve speech intelligibility, vowel contrast, and long-term motor recovery in clinical populations.

23.name: H-Reflex

top_papers.title: Activity-dependent spinal cord plasticity in health and disease
Locomotor capacity attributable to step training versus spontaneous recovery after spinalization in adult cats
Plasticity of the spinal neural circuitry after injury
Spinal cord plasticity in acquisition and maintenance of motor skills
Acquisition of a simple motor skill: Task-dependent adaptation plus long-term change in the human soleus H-reflex

23.summary: This community investigates the role of spinal cord plasticity and reflex adaptations—particularly the H-reflex and F-wave—during motor learning, postural control, and rehabilitation. Researchers utilize paradigms such as backward treadmill walking, balance training (e.g., slacklining, beam walking), and targeted force control tasks, often measuring spinal excitability, muscle synergies, and afferent feedback integration. A recurring finding is that spinal reflexes dynamically adapt to task uncertainties and specific sensory inputs, demonstrating task-specific neuroplasticity that can be leveraged to improve motor function in healthy individuals and those with spinal cord injuries.
themes: Spinal cord plasticity and H-reflex adaptation

24.name: Music Performance

top_papers.title: Learning to play a melody: An fMRI study examining the formation of auditory-motor associations
Cross-modal plasticity of the motor cortex while listening to a rehearsed musical piece
The effect of early musical training on adult motor performance: Evidence for a sensitive period in motor learning
Skill acquisition in music performance: Relations between planning and temporal control
Conceptual and motor learning in music performance

24.summary: This community investigates the acquisition, maintenance, and neural mechanisms of sensorimotor skills in music and dance performance. Researchers utilize neuroimaging (fMRI, EEG), brain stimulation (TMS, tDCS), and behavioral paradigms like sequence learning and motion capture to compare experts, novices, and children. Recurring findings demonstrate that extensive practice and early training enhance neural plasticity, movement consistency, and auditory-motor integration, though expert training can also constrain sensorimotor flexibility and occasionally lead to task-specific motor disorders like dystonia.

25.name: Stroke: task-oriented therapy

top_papers.title: Infusing motor learning research into neurorehabilitation practice: A historical perspective with case exemplar from the accelerated skill acquisition program
Getting neurorehabilitation right: What can be learned from animal models?
Effect of a task-oriented rehabilitation program on upper extremity recovery following motor stroke the ICARE randomized clinical trial
Very early constraint-induced movement during stroke rehabilitation (VECTORS): A single-center RCT
A Motor Learning Model for Stroke Rehabilitation

25.summary: This community investigates motor rehabilitation and neuroplasticity following neurological injuries, primarily stroke, traumatic brain injury, and spinal cord injury. Researchers employ paradigms such as task-oriented practice, constraint-induced movement therapy, non-invasive brain stimulation (rTMS), and sensory feedback to improve upper limb dexterity, gait, and balance. Recurring findings indicate that high-dosage, task-specific training significantly enhances motor recovery, while adjuncts like vibratory feedback, rTMS, and adequate sleep further optimize motor learning and skill acquisition.

26.name: Developmental Dyslexia

top_papers.title: Close interrelation of motor development and cognitive development and of the cerebellum and prefrontal cortex.
Exercise, sports, and performance arts benefit cognition via a common process
Association of abnormal cerebellar activation with motor learning difficulties in dyslexic adults
The Psychology Experiment Building Language (PEBL) and PEBL Test Battery
Into the Square and out of the Box: The effects of Quadrato Motor Training on Creativity and Alpha Coherence

26.summary: This community investigates the functional and neurobiological interconnectedness of motor and cognitive development, with a specific focus on developmental dyslexia and other cognitive disorders. Researchers frequently employ sensorimotor interventions—such as Quadrato Motor Training and complex coordinative exercises—alongside fMRI, ERPs, and behavioral tasks to measure impacts on attention, executive function, and reading abilities. Recurring findings demonstrate that targeted motor training induces neuroplasticity in the cerebellum and prefrontal cortex, while individuals with dyslexia exhibit shared neural deficits in motor sequence learning and orthographic decoding, alongside difficulties in generalizing newly acquired procedural skills.

27.name: Stroke: neurostimulation

top_papers.title: Neural plasticity and bilateral movements: A rehabilitation approach for chronic stroke
Increase in hand muscle strength of stroke patients after somatosensory stimulation
Effects of somatosensory stimulation on use-dependent plasticity in chronic stroke
Somatosensory Stimulation Enhances the Effects of Training Functional Hand Tasks in Patients With Chronic Stroke
Plasticity of the human motor cortex and recovery from stroke

27.summary: This community investigates interventions to enhance motor recovery and neural plasticity following stroke. Researchers frequently pair active motor training paradigms—such as bilateral movement practice, treadmill training, or arm ability training—with neuromodulatory techniques like peripheral electrical stimulation, transcranial direct current stimulation (tDCS), and mirror visual feedback. Using neurophysiological measurement tools like fMRI, EEG, and transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS), recurring findings demonstrate that appropriately timed and dosed sensory or brain stimulation combined with motor training facilitates cortical reorganization, reduces interhemispheric asymmetry, and significantly improves post-stroke motor function.

28.name: Sonification

top_papers.title: Motor learning with augmented feedback: Modality-dependent behavioral and neural consequences
Real-time kinematic, temporospatial, and kinetic biofeedback during gait retraining in patients: A systematic review
Multimodal motion processing in area V5/MT: Evidence from an artificial class of audio-visual events
The effect of real-time auditory feedback on learning new characters
Auditory coding of human movement kinematics

28.summary: This community investigates the use of real-time movement sonification and audio-biofeedback to enhance motor learning, postural control, and gait rehabilitation. By transforming kinematic and dynamic data into acoustic signals, researchers develop wearable and clinical systems that provide concurrent augmented feedback for tasks ranging from stroke rehabilitation to sports training. Recurring findings indicate that auditory feedback leverages auditory-motor coupling to improve movement execution, successfully modulates gait asymmetries and postural sway, and promotes better long-term skill retention with less feedback-dependence compared to visual cues.

29.name: Multiple Sclerosis

top_papers.title: Learning from laboratory-induced falling: Long-term motor retention among older adults
Inoculation Against Falls: Rapid Adaptation by Young and Older Adults to Slips During Daily Activities
Learning to resist gait-slip falls: Long-term retention in community-dwelling older adults
Retention of adaptive control over varying intervals: Prevention of slip-induced backward balance loss during gait
Repeated-Slip Training: An Emerging Paradigm for Prevention of Slip-Related Falls Among Older Adults.

29.summary: This community investigates motor learning, postural control, and adaptive plasticity in healthy individuals and clinical populations, including those with multiple sclerosis, stroke, and vestibular deficits. Researchers predominantly utilize perturbation-based balance training paradigms—such as treadmill slips, surface translations, and waist-pulls—to evaluate the acquisition, retention, and transfer of fall-resisting skills. Recurring findings demonstrate that progressive and high-interference perturbation training significantly improves reactive stability and temporal control, though specific neurological deficits can impair the retention of spatial control, highlighting the role of altered cerebellar-neocortical connectivity in mediating adaptive motor responses.

30.name: Fundamental Motor Skills

top_papers.title: Evaluation of the Psychometric Properties of the Test of Gross Motor Development—Third Edition.
On the Problem of Motor Skill Development.
The association between motor skill competence and physical fitness in young adults
Actual and Perceived Motor Competence Levels of Belgian and United States Preschool Children.
A Narrative Review of Motor Competence in Children and Adolescents: What We Know and What We Need to Find Out

30.summary: This community investigates the assessment, development, and impact of fundamental motor skills and overall motor competence in children. Researchers frequently utilize standardized tools like the Test of Gross Motor Development (TGMD), alongside emerging AI-based video assessments, to evaluate locomotor and object control skills. Recurring findings demonstrate that structured physical education and sports interventions significantly improve motor skills, and that foundational motor competence is critical for learning complex sports skills and fostering positive self-perception.

31.name: Myoelectric Control

top_papers.title: Sensitivity of Smoothness Measures to Movement Duration, Amplitude, and Arrests.
Functional reorganization of upper-body movement after spinal cord injury
Sensory motor remapping of space in human-machine interfaces
Remapping residual coordination for controlling assistive devices and recovering motor functions
A body machine interface based on inertial sensors

31.summary: This community investigates how humans learn to control assistive devices, such as prostheses and robotic manipulators, using biological signals via myoelectric and body-machine interfaces. Researchers employ paradigms like proportional and pattern-recognition electromyography (EMG), virtual reality simulations, and varied control mappings (biomimetic versus arbitrary) to study user-machine co-adaptation and motor skill acquisition. Recurring findings demonstrate that targeted training effectively improves device control and alters underlying muscle activation patterns, while exposure to variable or arbitrary mappings can enhance exploratory behavior, robustness, and generalization of the learned motor skills.

32.name: Simulation

top_papers.title: Does simulation-based medical education with deliberate practice yield better results than traditional clinical education? A meta-analytic comparative review of the evidence
A critical review of simulation-based medical education research: 2003-2009
Effect of augmented visual feedback from a virtual reality simulation system on manual dexterity training
Teaching psychomotor skills in the twenty-first century: Revisiting and reviewing instructional approaches through the lens of contemporary literature
Learn, See, Practice, Prove, Do, Maintain: An Evidence-Based Pedagogical Framework for Procedural Skill Training in Medicine

32.summary: This community investigates the acquisition of clinical, procedural, and psychomotor skills in healthcare education using simulation-based training and virtual reality (VR). Researchers frequently employ paradigms such as deliberate practice, mental practice, automated real-time feedback, and structured instructional models like Peyton's four-step approach or flipped classrooms. Recurring findings demonstrate that simulation-based education, particularly when paired with targeted feedback and deliberate practice, is superior to traditional clinical teaching methods for initial skill acquisition, though long-term skill retention often requires ongoing maintenance.

33.name: Developmental Robotics

top_papers.title: Is imitation learning the route to humanoid robots?
Computational approaches to motor learning by imitation
Infant grasp learning: A computational model
Human-like adaptation of force and impedance in stable and unstable interactions
Policy search for motor primitives in robotics

33.summary: This community investigates how autonomous robots, particularly humanoids and musculoskeletal systems, can acquire complex motor skills without extensive pre-programming. Researchers employ bio-inspired developmental paradigms such as imitation learning, intrinsic motivation, and goal babbling to help robots bootstrap behaviors from unstructured exploration to skilled manipulation and tool use. Recurring findings demonstrate that integrating biologically plausible mechanisms—such as memory consolidation, basal ganglia-cerebellum models, and dynamic movement primitives—enables efficient sensorimotor mapping, robust inverse kinematics learning, and improved generalization in novel environments.

34.name: Cerebellum: balance & coordination

top_papers.title: Neurobiology of skill and habit learning
Rotorod sensorimotor learning in cerebellar mutant mice
Tissue plasminogen activator induction in purkinje neurons after cerebellar motor learning
Motor learning deficits in aged rats are correlated with loss of cerebellar noradrenergic function
Motor skills and motor learning in Lurcher mutant mice during aging

34.summary: This community investigates the role of the cerebellum, its neural pathways, and specific genetic or molecular factors in motor skill learning and coordination. Researchers primarily use rodent models—including transgenic, mutant, and lesioned mice or rats—assessed via behavioral paradigms like the rotarod, runway navigation, and eyeblink conditioning. Recurring findings demonstrate that cerebellar motor learning is highly dependent on age and genetic strain, and is significantly impaired by neurodegenerative conditions like Alzheimer's disease or targeted disruptions to cerebellar pathways, receptors, and specific genes.

35.name: Autism Spectrum Disorder

top_papers.title: Evidence for a deficit in procedural learning in children and adolescents with autism: Implications for cerebellar contribution
Behavioural and neural basis of anomalous motor learning in children with autism
Motor learning relies on integrated sensory inputs in ADHD, but over-selectively on proprioception in autism spectrum conditions
Current perspectives on motor functioning in infants, children, and adults with autism spectrum disorders
Motor abilities in autism: A review using a computational context

35.summary: This community investigates motor learning and control deficits in individuals with Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD), focusing on challenges with procedural learning, fine motor control, and dyspraxia. Researchers employ paradigms such as serial reaction time tasks, prism adaptation, precision gripping, and diffusion tensor imaging to assess sensorimotor integration and internal model formation. Recurring findings indicate that while autistic individuals can successfully acquire internal models and adapt to motor tasks, they frequently exhibit impaired memory consolidation, an over-reliance on visual feedback, and altered feedforward planning. These behavioral differences are consistently linked to atypical microstructural connectivity within cerebellar, somatosensory, and primary motor networks.

36.name: Manual Aiming

top_papers.title: A Sensorimotor Basis for Motor Learning: Evidence Indicating Specificity of Practice
Goal-Directed Aiming: Two Components but Multiple Processes
Specificity of practice: The case of powerlifting
Role of kinesthetic and spatial-visual abilities in perceptual-motor learning
Practice does not diminish the role of visual information in on-line control of a precision walking task: Support for the specificity of practice hypothesis

36.summary: This community investigates the neuro-behavioral mechanisms of manual aiming and tracking, focusing on how visual feedback and practice schedules influence motor learning and control. Researchers frequently utilize rapid aiming, pursuit tracking, and simulated marksmanship paradigms, measuring performance via kinematic analysis of primary and secondary submovements, eye-hand coordination, and EEG. A recurring finding is the "specificity of practice" principle, demonstrating that learning is highly dependent on the sensory conditions present during training, with visual afferent information often dominating other sources. Additionally, practice consistently leads to smoother movements characterized by lengthened initial ballistic submovements and reduced reliance on corrective adjustments.

37.name: Conditional Motor Learning

top_papers.title: Roles of glabrous skin receptors and sensorimotor memory in automatic control of precision grip when lifting rougher or more slippery objects
Comparison of learning-related neuronal activity in the dorsal premotor cortex and striatum
Arbitrary associations between antecedents and actions
Motor learning in monkeys (Macaca fascicularis) with lesions in motor thalamus
Premotor cortex and conditional motor learning in man

37.summary: This community investigates how the central nervous system learns, represents, and adapts motor behaviors, particularly focusing on arbitrary visuomotor mappings and dexterous object manipulation. Researchers frequently employ precision grip-and-lift paradigms, haptic robotic perturbations, and neuroimaging techniques to study anticipatory force scaling, bimanual coordination, and motor transfer. Recurring findings indicate that conditional motor learning relies on distributed cortico-striatal and parieto-premotor networks to encode spatial frameworks and motor memories, enabling humans to predictively adapt grip forces to various load types and visual cues even in the presence of neurodegenerative conditions like Parkinson's disease.

38.name: Cognitive-Motor Training

top_papers.title: The effects of cognitive-motor training interventions on executive functions in older people: A systematic review and meta-analysis
Influence of sequential vs. simultaneous dual-task exercise training on cognitive function in older adults
Training effects on motor-cognitive dual-task performance in older adults: A systematic review
The beneficial effects of different types of exercise interventions on motor and cognitive functions in older age: a systematic review
Interactive cognitive-motor step training improves cognitive risk factors of falling in older adults - A randomized controlled trial

38.summary: This community investigates the effects of simultaneous cognitive and motor training on physical and executive functioning across diverse populations, including older adults, stroke patients, athletes, and adolescents. Researchers frequently employ dual-task paradigms, exergames, and mixed-reality environments to assess outcomes like postural control, gait adaptability, and working memory. A recurring finding is that cognitive-motor dual-task training enhances both motor performance and cognitive abilities—such as inhibitory control and processing speed—more effectively than single-task interventions, often by promoting functional movement variability and adaptive neuroplasticity.

39.name: Worked Examples

top_papers.title: Cognitive skill acquisition
Cognitive load theory in health professional education: design principles and strategies
Structuring the transition from example study to problem solving in cognitive skill acquisition: A cognitive load perspective
Cognitive load theory and complex learning: Recent developments and future directions
Worked-out examples: Instructional explanations support learning by self-explanations

39.summary: This community investigates the acquisition, retention, and transfer of complex cognitive and sensorimotor skills, heavily utilizing Cognitive Load Theory to optimize instructional design. Researchers employ paradigms such as worked-out examples, whole-task versus part-task instruction, and virtual reality simulations across medical and technical domains. A recurring finding is the "expertise reversal effect," where instructional methods highly effective for novices can induce excessive cognitive load or redundancy, thereby impairing learning and performance in more experienced individuals.

40.name: Infants

top_papers.title: Motor Development: Embodied, Embedded, Enculturated, and Enabling
THREE-MONTH-OLD InfinityANTS CAN LEARN TASK-SPECIFIC PATTERNS OF INTERLIMB COORDINATION.
The development of goal-directed reaching in infants: hand trajectory formation and joint torque control
Movement training advances the emergence of reaching in infants born at less than 33 weeks of gestational age: a randomized clinical trial.
Teach to reach: The effects of active vs. passive reaching experiences on action and perception

40.summary: This community investigates the acquisition of early motor skills, such as reaching, grasping, and object exploration, in infants and children. Researchers frequently employ behavioral interventions like the "sticky mittens" paradigm and contingent reinforcement, alongside neuroimaging (EEG, fNIRS) and kinematic analysis, to track sensorimotor maturation. A recurring finding is that motor development is highly responsive to active and observational experience, with newly acquired motor skills—such as independent sitting or reaching—triggering developmental cascades that enhance broader cognitive and perceptual abilities while corresponding neural activity shifts from diffuse to focal patterns.

41.name: Muscle Activation

top_papers.title: Principles for learning single-joint movements - I. Enhanced performance by practice
Practice improves even the simplest movements
Time course and temporal order of changes in movement kinematics during learning of fast and accurate elbow flexions
Neuromuscular adaptation during skill acquisition on a two degree-of-freedom target-acquisition task: Isometric torque production
Principles for learning single-joint movements - II. Generalizing a learned behavior

41.summary: This community investigates the neuromuscular and biomechanical mechanisms underlying motor control, coordination, and learning in human movement. Researchers utilize electromyography (EMG), kinematic tracking, and computational modeling to analyze tasks ranging from rapid goal-directed reaching and isometric force production to rhythmic and cyclic movements. A recurring finding is that motor practice refines muscle activation timing—particularly in antagonist muscles—and enhances multi-joint synergies, allowing the nervous system to exploit kinematic redundancy and biomechanical advantages for optimal, task-dependent performance.

42.name: Ataxia

top_papers.title: Cerebellar control of balance and locomotion
Moving, sensing and learning with cerebellar damage
Intensive coordinative training improves motor performance in degenerative cerebellar disease
Consensus paper: Management of degenerative cerebellar disorders
Brain changes associated with postural training in patients with cerebellar degeneration: A voxel-based morphometry study

42.summary: This community investigates motor coordination deficits, postural imbalance, and impaired motor learning resulting from cerebellar degeneration, spinocerebellar ataxia, and stroke. Researchers employ paradigms like repetitive motor practice, exergame training, and noninvasive cerebellar stimulation, evaluating outcomes via clinical scales, kinematics, and neuroimaging techniques such as fNIRS and voxel-based morphometry. A recurring finding is that despite cerebellar damage, patients retain sufficient neuroplasticity to improve motor performance and postural control through structured training, often relying on compensatory neural remodeling in areas like the premotor cortex.

43.name: Muscle Synergies

top_papers.title: The neural origin of muscle synergies
Optimal isn’t good enough
Neuromechanical principles underlying movement modularity and their implications for rehabilitation
A neural basis for motor primitives in the spinal cord
Concerns and Issues in Studying and Assessing Motor Learning.

43.summary: This community investigates how the central nervous system simplifies complex movement and facilitates motor learning through muscle synergies, which are modular neural coordinative structures. Researchers employ methods such as electromyography with non-negative matrix factorization, musculoskeletal modeling, and deep reinforcement learning to analyze tasks like reaching and exoskeleton-assisted walking. Recurring findings indicate that synergies naturally emerge to optimize energy and error, the fundamental number of modules remains stable across varying physical conditions, and humans adapt more rapidly to kinematic perturbations that are compatible with pre-existing motor modules.

44.name: Handwriting

top_papers.title: Learning through hand- or typewriting influences visual recognition of new graphic shapes: Behavioral and functional imaging evidence
The role of sensorimotor learning in the perception of letter-like forms: Tracking the causes of neural specialization for letters
Effects of different types of learning on handwriting movements in young children
Reading depends on writing, in Chinese
Handwriting or typewriting? The influence of pen-or keyboard-based writing training on reading and writing performance in preschool children

44.summary: This community investigates the acquisition, neural representation, and developmental mechanics of fine motor skills, with a primary focus on handwriting and letter recognition. Researchers utilize computerized kinematic assessments, neuroimaging (fMRI, diffusion tractography), and multisensory training paradigms (haptic guidance, visual-motor enrichment) across children, healthy adults, and populations with motor delays. Recurring findings demonstrate a strong bidirectional link between motor execution and visual recognition, showing that physical handwriting practice enhances letter recognition and reading skills more effectively than non-motor methods like typing. Additionally, studies reveal that these manual skills rely on effector-independent structural learning, specific white matter microstructures, and experience-driven cortical reorganization.

45.name: Retrieval-Induced Forgetting

top_papers.title: Preselection in short-term motor memory
The temporal placement of interpolated movements in short-term motor memory
Preselection and response biasing in short-term motor memory
Categorization by movement direction: Retrieval-induced forgetting of motor sequences grouped by motion features.
The Effect of Interpolated Motor Activities in Short-Term Motor Memory1

top_keywords.keyword: retroactive vs. proactive interference, short-term motor memory, college students

45.summary: This community investigates the cognitive mechanisms underlying short-term motor memory and motor skill acquisition, focusing on how movements are encoded, retained, and retrieved. Researchers utilize paradigms such as blindfolded linear arm-positioning, intermanual transfer, and complex motion pattern learning (e.g., dance figures) to manipulate practice levels, sensory feedback, and retention intervals. Recurring findings demonstrate that motor memory is highly plastic, characterized by phenomena like retrieval-induced forgetting, the early dominance of visual-spatial coding over motor coding, and the ability of subjects to develop internal error-detection mechanisms without extrinsic knowledge of results.

46.name: Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation

top_papers.title: Motor skill acquisition
The use of CPR feedback/prompt devices during training and CPR performance: A systematic review
Skill acquisition and retention in automated external defibrillator (AED) use and CPR by lay responders: A prospective study
An evaluation of objective feedback in basic life support (BLS) training
Trials of teaching methods in basic life support (3): Comparison of simulated CPR performance after first training and at 6 months, with a note on the value of re-training

46.summary: This community investigates methods to improve the acquisition and long-term retention of cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) and basic life support skills among laypeople and healthcare professionals. Researchers frequently use randomized controlled trials and systematic reviews to compare traditional instructor-led training against alternative paradigms, such as simplified continuous-compression protocols, staged teaching, and computer-based courses using manikins with real-time corrective feedback. Recurring findings demonstrate that objective feedback devices and simplified training models significantly enhance critical performance metrics like chest compression depth and ventilation volume, while individual factors like age, education, and perceived competence also mediate overall skill retention.

47.name: Qeeg

top_papers.title: Ankle dorsiflexion as an fMRI paradigm to assay motor control for walking during rehabilitation
The supplementary motor area in the cerebral cortex
Level of participation in robotic-assisted treadmill walking modulates midline sensorimotor EEG rhythms in able-bodied subjects
Mirror gait retraining for the treatment of patellofemoral pain in female runners
Exercise-mediated locomotor recovery and lower-limb neuroplasticity after stroke

47.summary: This community investigates the neural correlates of motor learning, adaptation, and rehabilitation across healthy individuals and clinical populations, including those with stroke, Parkinson's disease, and leprosy. Researchers employ neuroimaging and electrophysiological methods—primarily EEG, qEEG, ERPs, and fMRI—alongside paradigms like visual biofeedback, functional electrical stimulation, and user-driven treadmills to track cognitive-motor states. Recurring findings demonstrate that motor skill acquisition and physical rehabilitation induce measurable cortical plasticity, such as altered EEG phase synchronization, increased attentional reserve, and specific regional desynchronization, which directly correlate with improved kinematics and gait performance.

48.name: Previous Trial Effect

top_papers.title: Motor Learning: Passing a Skill from One Hand to the Other
Brain lateralisation and motor learning: selective effects of dominant and non-dominant hand practice on the early acquisition of throwing skills.
Intermanual transfer of force control is modulated by asymmetry of muscular strength
Acquisition of a complex basketball-dribbling task in school children as a function of bilateral practice order
Sequential effects after practice with the dominant and non-dominant hand on the acquisition of a sliding task in schoolchildren

48.summary: This community investigates how motor skills and movement information transfer between limbs (intermanual transfer) and across consecutive movements (the previous trial effect). Researchers employ unimanual and bimanual paradigms—such as force production, sequential reaching, and bimanual unloading—often utilizing transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS) or studying specific populations like stroke survivors and callosotomy patients. Recurring findings demonstrate that recent motor history significantly influences subsequent motor execution, and that well-learned bimanual actions can operate independently of callosal mediation whereas novel tasks require cross-hemispheric interaction.

49.name: Robotic Surgery

top_papers.title: Robotic surgery training and performance: Identifying objective variables for quantifying the extent of proficiency
Current state of virtual reality simulation in robotic surgery training: a review
Training and learning robotic surgery, time for a more structured approach: a systematic review

Learning curve using robotic surgery

49.summary: This community investigates methods for optimizing surgical training and skill acquisition in robot-assisted surgery, primarily utilizing the da Vinci Surgical System. Researchers evaluate virtual reality (VR) simulators, augmented reality (AR) environments, and dual-console setups against traditional laparoscopic training using standardized metrics like GEARS scores and cognitive load assessments. Recurring findings demonstrate that robotic platforms and structured, proficiency-based simulation curricula significantly accelerate learning curves, enhance bimanual dexterity, and improve operative safety compared to conventional methods.

50.name: Self-Regulation

top_papers.title: Intensive baseball practice improves the Go/Nogo reaction time, but not the simple reaction time
Self-regulated learning and expertise development in sport: current status, challenges, and future opportunities
Performance and human factors: Considerations about cognition and attention for self-paced and externally-paced events
Self-regulated learning of a motoric skill: The role of goal setting and self-monitoring
Self-regulation of motoric learning: A strategic cycle view

50.summary: This community investigates how cognitive, emotional, and strategic self-regulation influences motor skill acquisition and athletic performance. Researchers utilize paradigms such as Go/No-go tasks, qualitative interviews, and goal-setting interventions to assess inhibitory control, decision-making, and self-regulated learning across diverse populations, from infants to elite athletes. Recurring findings demonstrate that structured self-regulatory processes—including dynamic goal setting, self-evaluation, and emotional regulation—significantly enhance motor learning, motivation, and the ability to rapidly suppress or adapt planned actions in dynamic environments.

51.name: Perceptual Motor Learning

top_papers.title: My body in the brain: A neurocognitive model of body-ownership
The Rubber Hand Illusion: Feeling of Ownership and Proprioceptive Drift Do Not Go Hand in Hand.

From Part- to Whole-Body Ownership in the Multisensory Brain
Effects of a mirror-induced visual illusion on a reaching task in stroke patients: Implications for mirror therapy training

51.summary: This community investigates the neurocognitive mechanisms underlying bodily self-consciousness, body ownership, and motor coordination through multisensory integration. Researchers frequently employ paradigms like the Rubber Hand Illusion, virtual avatars, and robotic hands to manipulate visuo-tactile and visuomotor synchrony. A recurring finding is that altered visual and tactile feedback can recalibrate proprioceptive maps, induce unconscious muscular adaptations, and shift perceived body ownership, offering potential applications for motor rehabilitation and understanding the development of the bodily self.

52.name: Dreyfus Model

top_papers.title: The five-stage model of adult skill acquisition
Using the dreyfus model of skill acquisition to describe and interpret skill acquisition and clinical judgment in nursing practice and education

Intuition as a function of the expert nurse: a critique of Benner's novice to expert model

52.summary: This community focuses on the application of the Dreyfus five-stage model of skill acquisition to assess and improve clinical training across healthcare professions, including medicine, nursing, and pharmacy. Researchers utilize modified Dreyfus scales, simulation training, and performance rating rubrics to track learners' progression from novice to expert. Recurring findings demonstrate that these stage-based frameworks successfully correlate increased clinical exposure and simulation practice with measurable advancements in professional competency, providing a valid structure for standardized healthcare education.

53.name: Phenomenology

top_papers.title: Expertise in real world contexts
Skillful Coping in Everyday Life and in Sport: A Critical Examination of the Views of Heidegger and Dreyfus.
In praise of conscious awareness: A new framework for the investigation of "continuous improvement" in expert athletes
Automaticity in Skill Acquisition: Mechanisms for Reducing Interference in Concurrent Performance
Expertise and the evolution of consciousness

53.summary: This community investigates the acquisition, maintenance, and continuous improvement of expert motor skills, frequently challenging traditional theories that view expertise as strictly automatic and disrupted by conscious thought. Utilizing phenomenological and philosophical frameworks (such as those of Bergson, Polanyi, and Dewey) alongside cognitive experiments, researchers explore how conscious awareness, mindfulness, mental imagery, and somatic education influence athletic and artistic performance. Recurring findings indicate that experts strategically alternate between intuitive, automatic responses and reflective awareness to refine skills, overcome bodily crises like injury or aging, and optimize learning through adaptive strategies and distributed practice.

54.name: Virtual Co-Embodiment

top_papers.title: Effects of Collaborative Training Using Virtual Co-embodiment on Motor Skill Learning
Technological change and the skill acquisition of young workers
Development of a Moodle course for schoolchildren's table tennis learning based on competence motivation theory: Its effectiveness in comparison to traditional training method
Rotational perspective and learning procedural tasks from dynamic media
Applying augmented reality in physical education on motor skills learning

54.summary: This community investigates the use of advanced technologies and embodied cognition paradigms to enhance motor skill learning and physical education. Researchers employ methods such as virtual reality (including virtual co-embodiment), augmented reality, real-time pose recognition, and dynamic video modeling to address the limitations of static or passive instructional media. Recurring findings demonstrate that interactive, perspective-matched, and embodied interventions not only improve motor skill retention and performance across varying difficulty levels, but also boost learner motivation and facilitate cognitive processes like language comprehension.

55.name: White Matter Activation

top_papers.title: Brain changes following four weeks of unimanual motor training: Evidence from fMRI-guided diffusion MRI tractography
Short frontal lobe connections of the human brain
Diffusion tensor MRI tractography reveals increased fractional anisotropy (FA) in arcuate fasciculus following music-cued motor training
A neuronal model of a global workspace in effortful cognitive tasks
Plasticity in deep and superficial white matter: a DTI study in world class gymnasts

55.summary: This community investigates the structural and functional neuroplasticity induced by motor learning, skill acquisition, and goal-directed behaviors. Researchers utilize neuroimaging techniques like diffusion MRI, fMRI, and PET, alongside brain stimulation, to track neural changes during paradigms such as sequence training, eyeblink conditioning, and sports practice. Recurring findings demonstrate that intensive motor training drives significant white matter and connectivity adaptations in the cerebellum, corticospinal tract, and prefrontal cortex, while the baseline structural properties of regions like the parietal and frontal lobes can predict an individual's learning success and suitability for virtual reality.

56.name: Prism Adaptation

top_papers.title: Cognitive bias induced by visuo-motor adaptation to prisms: A simulation of unilateral neglect in normal individuals?
Rehabilitation of spatial neglect by prism adaptation. A peculiar expansion of sensorimotor after-effects to spatial cognition.
Simulating unilateral neglect in normals using prism adaptation: Implications for theory
Asymmetrical after-effects of prism adaptation during goal oriented locomotion
Sensorimotor effects on central space representation: Prism adaptation influences haptic and visual representations in normal subjects

56.summary: This community investigates visuo-motor plasticity and spatial cognition using the prism adaptation paradigm, where individuals perform motor tasks while wearing optically shifting lenses. Researchers frequently measure the perceptual and motor aftereffects of this adaptation using tasks like line bisection, visual search, and double-step saccades to understand spatial remapping. A recurring finding is that rightward-shifting prisms can alleviate spatial neglect in brain-damaged patients by modulating parietal attention networks, whereas leftward-shifting prisms induce neglect-like spatial biases in healthy individuals. Furthermore, these sensory-motor recalibrations can generalize to other domains, such as improving gait initiation in Parkinson's disease.

57.name: Learning & Memory

top_papers.title: Acquisition of motor skill: II. Rotary pursuit performance with continuous practice before and after a single rest
An experimental test of a two-factor theory of inhibition
Acquisition of motor skill: I. Quantitative analysis and theoretical formulation
Performance and reminiscence in motor learning as a function of the degree of distribution of practice
The effect of motivation and amount of pre-rest practice upon inhibitory potential in motor learning

57.summary: This community investigates the effects of practice distribution and rest intervals on motor skill acquisition, retention, and relearning. Researchers frequently employ tasks such as the pursuit rotor, stabilometer, and inverted alphabet printing to compare massed versus spaced practice paradigms and evaluate individual differences. Recurring findings demonstrate that distributed practice generally yields superior initial performance compared to massed practice, and that phenomena like reminiscence, reactive inhibition, and warm-up decrement operate consistently across both fine and gross motor tasks.
